topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a cold, heavy stone. It was from the publisher, a formal notification detailing the publication of my piece on urban gardening. My stomach tightened, a familiar knot forming just beneath my ribs. The name attached to the byline was wrong. It was *Elias Vance*. My name was Sarah Jenkins. I reread the text, the words I had poured my late nights into, now belonging to a stranger. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my hands tremble slightly as I stared at the screen. I felt a profound disconnect, as if I were watching a stranger navigate a life I was supposed to be living. I spent the next hour pacing my small apartment, the worn rug feeling suddenly too small, unable to settle into any position. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I opened the PDF, and the realization hit me with a dull, heavy thud. My carefully crafted narrative, the one that felt so intrinsically mine, was printed under another author’s moniker. A slow, creeping paralysis set in. I picked up my phone, intending to call the editor, but my fingers hovered over the dial pad, paralyzed by a strange inertia. My breath hitched in my throat, shallow and quick. I looked around my workspace—the stacks of unread manuscripts seemed to mock me, monuments to potential that remained untouched. I began methodically cleaning my desk, stacking papers with an almost obsessive precision, a desperate attempt to impose order on the swirling chaos inside my chest. Each movement was stiff, deliberate, fueled by a deep, unsettling emptiness.

[story 3]

The notification was stark, brutally simple: my article, "The Concrete Bloom," was live, attributed to Elias Vance. I stared at the screen until the pixels swam. A hollow ache settled deep in my chest, a sensation of being suspended in thick, resistant water. I walked to the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. I didn't feel angry; anger required energy I simply didn't possess right now. Instead, a profound weariness washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I sank onto the edge of my bed, pulling my knees to my chest, wrapping my arms around myself as if trying to hold myself together. The air felt too thick to draw in, and the simple act of breathing felt like an enormous, exhausting effort.
